1: wayyēreḏ yĕhûḏāh mēʾēt ʾeḥāyw wayyēṭ ʿaḏ-ʾîš ʿăḏullāmî — "Judah went down from his brothers." Wayyēreḏ (Qal of yāraḏ, H3381, to go down) — the descent-motif that pervades the Joseph narrative: Joseph was taken down to Egypt (Genesis 37:25; Genesis 39:1), Judah now goes down from his brothers. The chapter is a narrative interruption — placed between Joseph being sold (Genesis 37:36) and Joseph in Egypt (Genesis 39:1) — and the temporal marker baʿēt hahîʾ (at that time) links it to the broader Joseph story rather than placing it in a different era. Judah's "going down" and his separation from his brothers contrast with Joseph's parallel descent; both will converge at the covenant level through the Perez/Davidic line (v29).
2: wayyarʾ-šām yĕhûḏāh bat-ʾîš kĕnaʿănî ûšĕmô šûaʿ wayyiqqāḥeyhā wayyāḇōʾ ʾēleyhā — "Judah saw there the daughter of a Canaanite man named Shua, and took her and went in to her." Judah marries a Canaanite woman — the covenant boundary the patriarchs consistently negotiated: Abraham sent for a wife from Aram for Isaac (Genesis 24), Isaac charged Jacob not to marry a Canaanite (Genesis 28:1), Esau's Hittite wives were a grief to his parents (Genesis 26:34-35). Judah's marriage to a Canaanite is not editorially condemned here, but the consequences (Er, Onan, Shelah, and the Tamar crisis) flow from a household built outside the covenant pattern. The verb lāqaḥ (took) — the same taking-verb used for Shechem's assault in Genesis 34:2 — occurs without courtship narrative here.
3: waṯṯahar waṯṯēled bēn wayyiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmô ʿēr — "She conceived and bore a son, and he named him Er." The birth formula — conceive/bear/name — echoes the tribal-birth records of Genesis 30. ʿĒr (H6147) — the name's etymology is uncertain; it may simply be a family name. The rapid birth sequence (vv3-5, three sons in three verses) compresses the family history to set up the Tamar crisis; the sons matter narratively only in relation to the covenant question of Judah's continuation.
4: waṯṯahar ʿôḏ waṯṯēled bēn waṯṯiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmô ʾônān — "She conceived again and bore a son and named him Onan." ʾÔnān (H209) — possibly from ʾôn (H205, iniquity/trouble/sorrow) or from the same root as ʾawn (vigor, potency — used in Deuteronomy 21:17 for the firstborn's "firstfruits of his strength"). The birth record is minimal; Onan's significance is entirely defined by his act in v9 and the name's subsequent associations.
5: waṯṯōsep ʿôḏ waṯṯēled bēn waṯṯiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmô šēlāh wĕhāyāh biḵzîḇ bĕliddtāh ʾōtô — "She gave birth to yet another son and named him Shelah; she was at Chezib when she bore him." Šēlāh (H7956) — from an uncertain root; possibly related to šālaḥ (to send) or šelāh (petition). Kĕzîḇ (Chezib, H3580) — a place-name in the Shephelah, possibly the same as Achzib (Joshua 15:44; Micah 1:14); its name means "deceitful/lying" (kāzaḇ, H3576) — a narrative irony given the deception that will follow in this chapter.
6: wayyiqqaḥ yĕhûḏāh ʾiššāh lĕʿēr bĕḵôrô ûšĕmāh tāmār — "Judah got a wife for Er his firstborn; her name was Tamar." Tāmār (H8559, palm tree — the date palm, Phoenix dactylifera) — a name connoting beauty, uprightness, and fruitfulness; the same name as David's daughter Tamar (2 Samuel 13:1) and Absalom's daughter (2 Samuel 14:27). Palm trees were symbols of the righteous in Psalms 92:12 ("the righteous shall flourish like a palm tree"). Tamar enters the narrative named but voiceless — she does not speak until v25. The name is her characterization before her character is demonstrated.
7: wayhî ʿēr bĕḵôr yĕhûḏāh rāʿ bĕʿênê YHWH wayyĕmîtēhû YHWH — "But Er, Judah's firstborn, was wicked in the eyes of YHWH; and YHWH put him to death." The formula rāʿ bĕʿênê YHWH (wicked in YHWH's eyes, H7451) is the covenant judgment formula — used for Onan in v10, for the inhabitants of Sodom (Genesis 13:13), for Israel's kings (1 Kings 15:26; Genesis 16:25; 2 Kings 21:2). Wayyĕmîtēhû YHWH — "and YHWH put him to death" — a blunt statement of direct divine execution; Er's wickedness is not specified, which keeps the narrative focus on Tamar's subsequent predicament rather than on Er's sin. The divine judgment creates the Levirate obligation.
8: wayyōʾmer yĕhûḏāh lĕʾônān bōʾ ʾel-ʾēšet ʾāḥîḵā wĕyabbēm ʾōtāh wĕhāqēm zeraʿ lĕʾāḥîḵā — "Judah said to Onan, 'Go in to your brother's wife and perform the duty of a brother-in-law for her; raise up offspring for your brother.'" The verb yibbēm (H2992, to perform the Levirate duty, to marry the deceased brother's wife) — the technical term for what Deuteronomy 25:5-10 will later codify as yibbum law. The obligation: the surviving brother marries the widow to raise up the zĕraʿ (seed/offspring, H2233) for the dead brother — preserving his name and inheritance in Israel. The Levirate institution is pre-Sinaitic here; Judah invokes it as a known obligation without legal citation. Hāqēm zĕraʿ (raise up seed) — the language of covenant continuation.
9: wayyēḏaʿ ʾônān kî lōʾ lô yihyeh hazzāraʿ wĕhāyāh ʾim-bāʾ ʾel-ʾēšet ʾāḥîw wĕšiḥēt ʾarṣāh lĕḇiltî nĕtōn-zĕraʿ lĕʾāḥîw — "Onan knew the offspring would not be his; so whenever he slept with his brother's wife, he wasted it (šiḥēt) on the ground, so as not to give offspring to his brother." Šiḥēt (Hiphil of šāḥat, H7843, to destroy, corrupt, ruin, waste) — the verb for moral and physical corruption: the golden calf is described with šāḥat (Exodus 32:7: "your people have corrupted themselves"); the flood is sent because the earth was šāḥat (Genesis 6:11-12). Onan does not merely "waste" the seed in a neutral sense — he actively "corrupts" or "ruins" the covenantal seed. The act is an act of destruction directed against his brother's covenant line.
10: wayyēraʿ bĕʿênê YHWH ʾăšer ʿāśāh wayyāmēt gam-ʾōtô — "What he did was wicked in the eyes of YHWH; so he put him to death also." The judgment formula is verbally identical to v7 with the addition of gam-ʾōtô (him also) — emphasizing that both brothers have been executed by YHWH for wickedness. The parallelism of vv7 and 10 creates a narrative pattern that prepares the reader to understand Judah's fear in v11: two sons have already died married to Tamar; Judah calculates (wrongly) that she is the cause rather than their wickedness.
11: wayyōʾmer yĕhûḏāh lĕtāmār kallātô šĕḇî ʾalmānāh ḇêt-ʾāḇîḵ ʿaḏ-yiggĕḏal šēlāh ḇĕnî kî ʾāmar pen-yāmût gam-hûʾ kĕʾeḥāyw — "Judah said to his daughter-in-law Tamar, 'Remain a widow in your father's house until my son Shelah grows up' — for he thought, 'He may die too, just like his brothers.'" ʾAlmānāh (H490, widow, one who is silent/bereft — from ʾālam, to be silent) — the widow in biblical law was among the most vulnerable members of society (Deuteronomy 10:18; Genesis 24:17-21; Exodus 22:22-24). Judah's instruction to Tamar is a legal limbo: she is released from the household but not given Shelah — effectively stranded as a widow without recourse. The narrator's disclosure of Judah's interior motive (v11b) makes clear this is protective pretext, not genuine fulfillment of the Levirate obligation.
12: wayyirbu hayyāmîm waṯṯāmāṯ bat-šûaʿ ʾēšet yĕhûḏāh wayyinnāḥem yĕhûḏāh wayyaʿal ʿal-gōzĕzê ṣōʾnô hûʾ wĕhîrāh rēʿēhû hāʿăḏullāmî timnatāh — "After a long time Judah's wife died. After Judah was comforted, he went up to his sheepshearers at Timnah." Wayyirbu hayyāmîm (the days multiplied/many days passed) — the time-compression formula for waiting. Judah's recovery from grief (wayyinnāḥem, Niphal of nāḥam, H5162, to be comforted, relent) leads directly to the sheep-shearing trip. Sheep shearing (gēzez ṣōʾn) was a festival occasion in the ancient world — associated with feasting and celebratory behavior (cf. 1 Samuel 25:4-8 for Nabal's shearing feast). Judah's movement "up" (wayyaʿal) to Timnah while Tamar acts to intercept him is the narrative setup.
13: wayyuggad lĕtāmār lēʾmōr hinnēh ḥāmîḵ ʿōleh timnatāh liggōz ṣōʾnô — "Tamar was told, 'Your father-in-law is on his way up to Timnah to shear his sheep.'" Tamar's being informed by an unnamed messenger (wayyuggad — Hophal: it was told) — the passive form suggests a community network of information. Tamar has been waiting in her father's house without Shelah being given to her; the sheep-shearing occasion presents the opportunity for action. The narrator presents Tamar as a strategic agent responding to information: she has been denied her legal right, and she will act to claim it through the only avenue available.
14: waṯṯāsar bigḏê ʾalmĕnûtāh mēʿāleyhā waṯṯĕḵas baṣṣāʿîp waṯṯitʿallāp waṯṯēšeḇ bĕpetaḥ ʿênayim ʾăšer ʿal-dereḵ timnatāh — "She removed her widow's garments and covered herself with a veil to disguise herself." Bigḏê ʾalmĕnûtāh (widow's garments, from ʾalmānāh) — the clothing of social status; removing them is an act of identity-disguise. Ṣāʿîp (H6809, veil/wrapper) — a large covering garment that obscures identity. Waṯṯitʿallāp (wrapped herself, Hithpael of ʿālap, H5968, to cover/veil oneself) — she actively performs the disguise. Petaḥ ʿênayim (opening of the eyes/Enaim) — possibly a place name meaning "two springs" or "crossroads"; the narrator's note that it was "on the road to Timnah" makes Judah's encounter unavoidable. Her motive is explicitly stated: Shelah had grown up and she had not been given to him.
15: wayyirʾehā yĕhûḏāh wayyaḥšĕḇehā lĕzônāh kî kissĕtāh pānêhā — "Judah saw her and took her for a prostitute (zônāh), for she had covered her face." Zônāh (H2181, harlot/prostitute — Qal participle of zānāh, to commit sexual immorality) — Judah's assumption based on the covered face and the roadside position. Notably, Judah will use a different word in v21 when he instructs Hirah to find her: qĕḏēšāh (H6948, a consecrated/dedicated woman — a cultic term for a woman devoted to a deity, often associated with temple prostitution in ANE practice). Hirah uses the more socially respectable term; the men of the place say there was no qĕḏēšāh there. The distinction between zônāh (ordinary prostitute) and qĕḏēšāh (sacred devotee) reflects two different social-religious categories, and Judah/Hirah's word choice tracks this distinction.
16: wayyēṭ ʾēleyhā ʾel-haddereḵ wayyōʾmer hābāh-nāʾ ʾāḇôʾāh ʾēlayin kî lōʾ yāḏaʿ kî kallātô hîʾ — "He turned aside to her on the road and said, 'Come now, let me come to you' — for he did not know she was his daughter-in-law." The narrator's moral-ironic note (kî lōʾ yāḏaʿ kî kallātô hîʾ — he did not know she was his daughter-in-law) is repeated at v26 for structural emphasis. The unknowing frames Judah's act as unconscious but not innocent — he is propositioning a woman at a roadside, which is itself the moral problem. The subsequent recognition in v26 will require him to acknowledge the double-irony: the woman he violated is more righteous than he is.
17: wayyōʾmer ʾānōḵî ʾăšallaḥ gĕḏî-ʿizzîm min-haṣṣōʾn waṯṯōʾmer ʾim-tittēn ʿērāḇôn ʿaḏ šolḥeḵā — "'I will send a young goat from the flock,' he said. 'Will you give a pledge until you send it?'" Gĕḏî-ʿizzîm (kid/young goat from the goat-flock) — the same animal used for Jacob's deception of Isaac (Genesis 27:9: goat-kids for the savory dish; Genesis 27:16: goat-skins for the arm). ʿĒrāḇôn (H6162, pledge/security deposit) — a commercial-legal term used only here in the OT (vv17,18,20); cognate with the Akkadian erābū and Greek arrabon (Ephesians 1:14: "the Spirit as the pledge/deposit of our inheritance"). The pledge transforms a transactional encounter into a legally binding arrangement — Judah must reclaim his identifying items or remain legally accountable.
18: wayyōʾmer mah-hāʿērāḇôn ʾăšer ʾettēn-lāḵ waṯṯōʾmer ḥôtāmĕḵā ûpĕtîleḵā ûmaṭṭĕḵā ʾăšer bĕyāḏeḵā — "She answered, 'Your seal, your cord, and your staff in your hand.'" Ḥôtām (H2368, signet seal — a cylinder seal or stamp seal worn on a cord used to authenticate documents and ownership) + pĕtîl (H6616, cord/thread/bracelet — the cord on which the seal hangs) + maṭṭeh (H4294, staff/rod — also the word for a tribal staff/scepter). These three items are Judah's most personally identifying possessions — the equivalent of modern ID documents. Tamar's shrewd choice of pledge items ensures that when the truth is revealed, the evidence is irrefutable. The maṭṭeh (staff) in particular carries the weight of tribal authority: the same word is used for Jacob's tribal staffs (Genesis 30) and for Moses's staff before Pharaoh.
19: waṯṯāqom waṯṯēleḵ waṯṯāsar ṣāʿîpāh mēʿāleyhā waṯṯilḇaš bigḏê ʾalmĕnûtāh — "She rose and went, removing her veil and putting on her widow's garments again." The reversal is complete: the widow's garments come off for the disguise; they go back on afterward. Tamar's act is not permanent abandonment of her status but a targeted intervention. The garment-change as identity-transformation is a recurring Genesis motif (Joseph's coat of many colors: status; Rebekah's disguising Jacob; Jacob changing clothes for Bethel in Genesis 35:2); here it is Tamar who controls the garment-sign.
20: wayyišlaḥ yĕhûḏāh ʾet-gĕḏî hāʿizzîm bĕyaḏ rēʿēhû hāʿăḏullāmî lāqaḥat hāʿērāḇôn miyyaḏ hāʾiššāh wĕlōʾ mĕṣāʾāh — "Judah sent the young goat by his friend the Adullamite, to recover the pledge from the woman, but he did not find her." The recovery attempt uses the intermediary Hirah, possibly because Judah is embarrassed to return in person. The failure to find her sets up the inquiry of v21 — Hirah asks for a qĕḏēšāh (shrine devotee) rather than a zônāh (prostitute), using the more dignified term even in this awkward errand.
21: wayyišʾal ʾet-ʾanĕšê mĕqōmāh lēʾmōr ʾayyēh haqqĕḏēšāh hîʾ ʿal-hāʿênayim ʿal-haddāreḵ wayyōʾmĕrû lōʾ-hāyĕtāh ḇāzeh qĕḏēšāh — "He asked the men of the place, 'Where is the shrine devotee (qĕḏēšāh) who was beside the road at Enaim?' 'There has been no shrine devotee here,' they said." Qĕḏēšāh (H6948, feminine of qāḏôš = holy/set-apart — but in this cultic context designating a woman dedicated to a deity, often associated with fertility cult in Canaanite religion) — Hirah's use of this term rather than zônāh (common prostitute) reflects either social courtesy or his belief that the veiled woman at the roadside was a cultic figure. The men's answer — there has been no qĕḏēšāh here — closes the inquiry. The evidence of the pledge items remains with Tamar.
22: wayyāšāḇ ʾel-yĕhûḏāh wayyōʾmer lōʾ mĕṣāʾtîhā wĕgam ʾanĕšê hammāqôm ʾāmĕrû lōʾ-hāyĕtāh ḇāzeh qĕḏēšāh — "He returned to Judah and reported, 'I didn't find her; and the men of the place said there has been no shrine devotee here.'" Hirah's report closes the pledge-recovery attempt. The word qĕḏēšāh appears twice in vv21-22 — both times by Hirah. The narrator has Judah use zônāh in v15 (his private assumption) and v23 (his frustrated resignation); Hirah consistently uses the more honorable term. The distinction traces the social distance between Judah's private act and its public presentation.
23: wayyōʾmer yĕhûḏāh tiqqaḥ-lāh pen niḇûz hinnēh šālaḥtî haggĕḏî hazzeh wĕʾattāh lōʾ mĕṣāʾtāh — "Judah said, 'Let her keep the pledge, or we will become a laughingstock (lĕḇûz). At least I sent the young goat, but you couldn't find her.'" Lĕḇûz (contempt/laughingstock, H937, from bāzāh, to despise) — Judah's concern is reputation: being known as the man who could not recover his pledge from a prostitute is humiliating. The shame-concern leads him to abandon the search. His words "at least I sent the goat" (šālaḥtî haggĕḏî) acknowledge his contractual obligation without resolving the unredeemed pledge — which remains in Tamar's possession as the evidence she needs.
24: wayhî kĕmiššĕlōšāh ḥŏḏāšîm wayyuggad lĕyĕhûḏāh lēʾmōr zāntāh tāmār kallāteḵā wĕgam hinnēh hārāh liznûnîm wayyōʾmer yĕhûḏāh hôṣîʾûhā wĕtissārēp — "About three months later Judah was told, 'Your daughter-in-law Tamar has played the harlot, and indeed she is pregnant from harlotry.' Judah said, 'Bring her out and burn her!'" The three-month interval allows the pregnancy to be visible and unmistakable. Judah's pronouncement of capital punishment — wĕtissārēp (let her be burned, Niphal of śārap, H8313, to burn) — reflects the penalty for a betrothed woman's sexual infidelity (Leviticus 21:9 prescribes burning specifically for the daughter of a priest; the broader principle of Deuteronomy 22:23-24 prescribes stoning). The irony is that Judah is pronouncing sentence on the very act he initiated — and the sentence reveals his moral double standard: he propositioned a roadside woman but expects his daughter-in-law to maintain sexual fidelity to a levirate promise he has not honored.
25: hîʾ mûṣĕʾāh wĕhîʾ šālĕḥāh ʾel-ḥāmîhā lēʾmōr lĕʾîš ʾăšer-ʾēlleh lô ʾānōḵî hārāh waṯṯōʾmer hakkēr-nāʾ lĕmî haḥōtāmet wĕhapĕtîlîm wĕhammaṭṭeh hāʾēlleh — "As she was brought out, she sent word to her father-in-law: 'I am pregnant by the man whose these are.' And she said, 'Recognize, please, whose seal, cord, and staff these are.'" Hakkēr-nāʾ (recognize/identify, please — Hiphil imperative of nāḵar, H5234, with the particle nāʾ) — this is the precise verbal formula used in 37:32 when Joseph's brothers brought his blood-stained coat to Jacob: hakkēr-nāʾ hakkĕtōnet bĭnĕḵā hîʾ (recognize please, is this your son's coat?). The same words by which Jacob was deceived about Joseph are now spoken to Judah about Tamar — a narrative wordplay linking the two deceptions. Tamar's method of disclosure is indirect — she sends the evidence rather than accusing directly, allowing Judah to make the identification himself.
26: wayyakkēr yĕhûḏāh wayyōʾmer ṣāḏĕqāh mimmennî kî-ʿal-kēn lōʾ-nĕtattîhā lĕšēlāh bĕnî wĕlōʾ-yāsap ʿôḏ lĕḏaʿtāh — "Judah recognized them and said, 'She is more righteous than I (ṣāḏĕqāh mimmennî), for I did not give her to my son Shelah.'" Ṣāḏĕqāh mimmennî — the Qal perfect of ṣāḏaq (H6663, to be righteous, to be in the right) with the comparative preposition min: "she is more in the right than I." This is one of the most direct moral self-indictments in Genesis — a patriarchal figure explicitly acknowledging a woman's superior righteousness. Judah names his own sin of omission: lōʾ nĕtattîhā lĕšēlāh (I did not give her to Shelah). Tamar acted under the pressure of a denied legal right; Judah acted out of self-interest. Wĕlōʾ-yāsap ʿôḏ lĕḏaʿtāh — "he knew her no more" — closing the sexual relationship permanently.
27: wayhî bĕʿēt liḏtāh wĕhinnēh tĕʾômîm bĕḇiṭnāh — "When it was time for her to give birth, behold, twins were in her womb." The exclamatory wĕhinnēh (and behold!) marks the surprise: a twin birth. Twin births in Genesis are theologically significant: Esau and Jacob (Genesis 25:24) were twins whose birth order was reversed; now Perez and Zerah's reversed emergence will replay the motif. The twin birth signals a covenant-structural event rather than a routine genealogy.
28: wayhî bĕliḏtāh wayyittēn-yāḏ waṯṯiqqaḥ hamĕyalleḏet waṯṯiqšōr ʿal-yāḏô šānî lēʾmōr zeh yāṣāʾ riʾšōnāh — "As she gave birth, one put out his hand; the midwife tied a scarlet thread (šānî) on his wrist and said, 'This one came out first.'" Šānî (H8144, scarlet/crimson) — the scarlet thread designating the firstborn echoes the scarlet thread that Rahab will hang from her window to mark her household for protection (Joshua 2:18-21). The scarlet thread as a marking-device that saves and identifies recurs in both texts, though the specific connections are typological rather than direct allusion.
29: wayhî kĕmēšîḇ yāḏô wĕhinnēh yāṣāʾ ʾāḥîw waṯṯōʾmer mah-pāraṣtā ʿāleḵā pāreṣ wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô pāreṣ — "But as he drew back his hand, his brother came out! She said, 'What a breaking-out you have broken! (mah-pāraṣtā ʿāleḵā pāreṣ).' So he was named Perez." Pāreṣ (H6557) from pāraṣ (H6555, to break through, burst out, make a breach) — the birth-reversal naming-speech: the one who seemed to come second burst through first. Pāraṣ is the verb of covenant-overflow: Genesis 28:14 (your offspring shall pāraṣ in all directions), 30:30 and 43 (Jacob's flocks pāraṣ). Perez is the direct ancestor of David (Ruth 4:18-22: "Perez begot Hezron... Obed begot Jesse... Jesse begot David") and of the Messiah (Matthew 1:3). The birth-reversal of the Tamar narrative — the unexpected supplanter rather than the designated heir — joins the chain of covenant-reversals in Genesis: Abel/Cain, Isaac/Ishmael, Jacob/Esau, Joseph/Reuben, Perez/Zerah.
30: wĕʾaḥar yāṣāʾ ʾāḥîw ʾăšer ʿal-yāḏô hašŝānî wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô zāraḥ — "Then his brother who had the scarlet thread on his wrist came out, and he was named Zerah." Zāraḥ (H2226) — possibly related to zāraḥ (H2224, to rise, shine — used for the sun's rising) or zārak (to sprinkle/scatter). The "shining one" who was marked first but born second — his name may encode the light of the scarlet thread that promised him precedence. Zerah appears in the genealogies (1 Chronicles 2:4,6) but it is Perez who carries the covenant line to David. The narrative principle is established: the thread of human expectation is not the thread of divine election.
